speaker: interviewer
text: But you can talk about it. I can-- I'll ask you what your name is. And you'll say, Walsh. And then you'll say, it was originally Waks. And here's why.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I won't bring in Walsh in it. It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 19, 1996. The survivor's name is Mr. Mike Walsh, W-A-L-S-H. He was born Mordechai Waks, W-A-K-S. I am the interviewer. My name is Richard Bassett. We are in Toronto, Canada. And the interview will be held in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 19, 1996. The survivor's name is Mr. Mike Walsh. He was born Mordechai Waks. I am the interviewer. My name is Richard Bassett, B-A-S-S-E-T-T. We are in Toronto, Canada. And the interview will be held in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mike Walsh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-K-E W-A-L-S-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that your original family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my name was Mordechai Waks at home, and my parents' name. But for some reason, in Germany, it was changed. And I never really changed it back. But I go by both names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born, Mr. Walsh?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the Ukraine, in a small village called Male Werbcze. And I was born in Purim, actually-- April 1, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Male Werbcze for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-L-E. The second word is V-E-R-B-C-Z-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Walsh, what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was-- my father's name was Shaul Waks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where he was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he was born in the same place, in the village. Because the family has lived for many years in Male Werbcze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many years, how many generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you how many generations. But I know that they were established many years and well-known in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was your father born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in 1887. That's what, approximately, I remember. And he said that he was born in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father was killed in 1942 after they took him from the ghetto with my-- my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was taken from the ghetto with my sister to [? Kochivle, ?] from the Stepan ghetto. And there they were doing the extermination of the Jews. My second brother, Yakov, was with them. But he escaped. And my sister had a chance to escape while they were go-- taking them for the killing, but she didn't want to leave my father alone. So she went along with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that your father was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some people that survived after the killing. There were a couple of people that were injured. And also, my brother, the one that escaped, it was only about 10 minutes before they arrived to the place where they did the extermination. And he escaped into the woods. So he knows that they took him there, with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a hazzan. And he was going from town to town on high holidays to do the prayers. And we had a little farm in the village-- a cow, a horse. And this is what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he earned his living from being a hazzan?

speaker: interviewee
text: A hazzan and the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you sell, from the farm, [? produce? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just the farm was just for our own use. And we were still buying from some neighbors. Before he-- when he was with his parents, they had an inn in the village. And they also had a tar factory, which they called Waks. And I presume that maybe the name they took on years ago that my grandfather had operated this factory for tar, which they called Waks. Maybe the name came from there. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your grandparents' names on your father's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's father name was Shabtai. And my grandmother's name was Yona-- [? Tibul ?] in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you actually remember them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember my father's grandparents. I remember my mother's grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name from my mother's side was Yeshayahu. And my grandmother's name was Chana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their family name was Taks, T-A-K-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you. I don't remember what my grandfather did. He seemed to be very-- they seemed to be very old. They used to come to visit then, at the time. And when I knew them, they were already, like, what you call old and retired. Or he was always going to shul. But that's all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Chaya.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that she was also-- she went with your father, together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When they took us to the ghetto, I was left in the village. And I took care of a child and helped a farmer, a neighbor. And at the same time, they left me there. And at the same time, I was actually bringing the food to the village. We had some clothing, and some other furniture, and so on. And my brother used to sell it to the people and then get some money and buy food. And he also worked. Yitshak didn't go to the ghetto either. Because I had an uncle that had a factory of, like, a paint. What do you call it-- sid-- what do-- you know, like to paint, such white stuff that they used to manufacture. And the Germans kept him and my uncle in the village, in this factory. And he used to earn a little bit of money. And he used to buy food. And I used to be dressed as a Ukrainian kid, and jump into the ghetto through the fence, and bring food for my parents. And we had to still be careful not to bring any meats or anything, because they were still kosher in the ghetto. Now you asked me about my mother. The day-- in 1942, in August '42, when they took us all to the [? Kochivle ?] killing grounds, that night, somehow I was lonesome, and I came to visit my parents. I was 12 years old. And that night exactly that I stayed with my parents, they started to gather us to take us to the killing. We were in a room in the house where we stayed, a little house. There were about eight families. Each one had a room, or in a room maybe two families. I was with my mother, my sister, and my father. My other brothers, three of them, were in the labor camp. And my younger second brother, Yakov, was in another section in the ghetto. Around 2 o'clock in the morning, they started to gather people. We hid in a-- in the attic. They put-- took all the people that they found. And around 2:30, 3 o'clock, probably close to 4 o'clock in the morning, they started to look for us-- the Germans and the Ukrainian Nazis. So we opened the window. And we started to jump. And my mother asked me to jump first. And then she jumped after me. And we just heard a lot of shooting in the room. And they-- my father and my sister couldn't escape anymore. The Germans came with the Ukrainian Nazis and picked them up. And my mother and I escaped nearby to a Ukrainian barn, a barn a Ukrainian farmer had. And we stayed there until daylight. And at daylight, came in the farmer and saw us, and chased us out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were on our way to go to our village. As we were going to the village, we just didn't remember. I used to go every week. But I got all upset. And I didn't remember the way back to the village, Male Werbcze. So we went into a farmer, not far from the Jewish cemetery in Stepan, and asked the way to go to Werbcze.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this farmer came up to us and says, oh, you are Jews. You killed Christ. You are communists. And you should die too-- took my mother and me by the shoulders. He didn't have a weapon or anything. And he just was leading us to the police station, where they were gathering probably the ones that escaped, and then taking them back for the extermination. We were walking. As we walked, we-- I recognized the road that goes to the village, Male Werbcze. And I said in Yiddish to my mother that this is where we go to Male Werbcze. My mother started to beg the Ukrainian, please, let us go. Let us go. He wouldn't. So she says, let the kid go and you can go with him. And we have buried some silver or whatever, some money near our house. And he will give you everything. And she told me where it was. He says, oh, no. No way. In that case, my mother said, try to escape, to me. Since I was really a village kid, I acted very fast. I fell down to the ground and started to run. The Ukrainian bandit didn't run after me. He took my mother. And I never saw her again. And I escaped into the forest. And then at night, I went to a close friend that I went to school, the mayor's son, the mayor of the village son. His name was Ivan. And he took care of me. And the father hid me. The next day, the mayor's-- the mayor went to the city of Stepan, to the town. And he wanted to see my mother. So he went down to the police station. And he saw my mother. He visited her, and talked to her, and told her in secret that I escaped and he will take care of me. And my mother said to send regards and she thanked him very much. And she said she hoped that I would stay alive for her sake, and I should take care of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this happened-- [COUGHS] all this happened when, in August of '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this all took place within a few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in a few days, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come back to this. I just wanted to ask you a little bit more about your childhood. Could you tell me, how many brothers and sisters were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were five brothers and one sister. The eldest brother-- his name was Shabtai.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born, from what I know, about 1914, Shabtai. And then my sister-- next was sister-- 1916. Her name was Sonia. And the next, my brother Yitshak-- he was born 1918. And then I had a brother Chaim. He was born in 1926. Yakov was born in 1928. And then myself-- I was born in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so was there anybody after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no others.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you were the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was the youngest, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the youngest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now which out of-- which out of the children, apart from yourself, survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the extermination, my brother Yakov escaped, like I said before, from the-- practically near the killing. He escaped into the village. And he survived then. Then my other two brothers, Chaim and Shabtai, were in a labor camp in a town of Kostopol, which was about 30 kilometers west from Stepan. These were all young Jewish men from the age of 16 and up were kept there in labor camps. During the night, when the extermination started in Kostopol of the labor camp, they all broke the barriers of the gates and some of the walls. 80% or 90% of them were all killed. But my brother Shabtai and Chaim escaped into the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into what village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the village of Male Werbcze, where we came from, because they knew there the people would help us out. So actually, all five of us survived for that time. We were all into hiding with our neighbors and friends in the village of Male Werbcze and the area. And nobody touched us. The people were really, I would say, good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which of you-- which of the children survived after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only surviving brother I have now is Yitshak, who lives in Israel with his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they-- did any of the other children pass on between the end of the war and-- and today? Or they were all-- were they all killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers were killed. One, Shabtai, was killed in 19-- the end of 1942, beg-- I think it was February-- sorry-- 1943. He was caught by some of the Ukrainian bandits and taken to the Germans. And he was killed. And another brother, Chaim, who was in the military, like, police force to take care of all the Nazis-- Ukrainian Nazis-- in the Soviet police-- he was killed in October 1944. And Yakov was also in the same police. He was killed by the Ukrainian Nazis in 1945-- sorry, '47, 1947, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Ukraine-- since he was in the police and he was after getting-- after those Nazis, they knew where he was staying. And they attacked the unit that he was with. And he was killed with some other police forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you lived in Male Werbcze, the address? Where are we at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived in a village. There was no address really, like, in there, no number. There was just a small village. The meaning of 'Male' means small. 'Werbcze,' which means-- just to explain-- 'werbcze' means--

speaker: interviewer
text: A willow?

speaker: interviewee
text: --willow trees. And they did have a lot of willows in there. And we lived in a house. And all I know is we had a big sign there, [? 'Shavil ?] Waks,' which if somebody was looking, he could see the name on the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is 'Shavil?' What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, in Ukrainian, it was [? 'Shavil.' ?] It  was 'Shaul' pronounced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. What did the house look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was made of wood. There was no foundation. It was sitting on the ground foundation with some-- like, made of logs. And the roof was made of straw, [LAUGHS] like in a story town. And I remember when I was five years old, there was a fire. Because I guess the house was heated with wood and a spark went on the roof. And since the roof was made of straw, the fire caught very fast. And we lost the house in-- in minutes. And a neighbor, a Ukrainian neighbor came and took me in. That I remember. Like I say, it was in 1935. And then we were taken care of by a Ukrainian neighbor, until my father got insurance. I was surprised, in those days, that they had insurance. But I do remember that he collected insurance. And we bought another house from another neighbor. I even remember his name. The name was Makar. And he sold us the house with a big, big stall in it. And then we lived until we were taken away, in this house, with a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this house near where the other one had been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just about a block away, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Makar his family name or his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the family name, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The neighbor that took you in at first-- do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately not. I do remember one name. It was across the road. He took care of us. Nichipor was his name. Patrikei-- his last name. And the lady that took me in-- she was actually the midwife that delivered me. And they called her, in Ukrainian or whatever-- Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. She was the one that delivered me when I was born. So I was in her place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms does this house have before it burned down?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had only a kitchen and-- I don't remember much about that house. But I do remember it was just like one room, the whole house. And the new house had the same thing. It had one bedroom, and the kitchen, and a living room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my family was all Orthodox. And my father, like I said, was a hazzan, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, we spoke Yiddish. And naturally, since we lived in a Ukrainian village, we spoke Ukrainian as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, in Male Werbcze, to a public school. And in the summer, we used to go to a cheder, where I started at the age of three. And since we were very few families in the village of Male Werbcze, there was another village called [? Vilica ?] Werbcze, which is the big village of Werbcze. And there they had more Jewish families. And they had a cheder there. And my father used to teach us in our own house with some other neighbors' kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of what your parents were like? What were their personalities like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to say. My mother seemed to be very good natured-- my father too, getting along very well with the neighbors. And from what I saw, the way the neighbors took care of us and helped us during the war to feed us, and helped us to-- to hide us from the Germans and the Nazis, I find it was really a legacy, which was left that the people really cared for us and because we cared for our neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents loud, quiet, funny, serious?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to say. Because their life was a very hard life. You worked day and night. My mother, with six kids, had to do all the housework, and wash the laundry in the river, and get the water from the well across in the village. So-- but we had a lot of entertainment with some of the Jewish neighbors, with the neighbors from the villages, my grandparents that lived in the next village, called [? Korist. ?] And also, a lot of the neighbors used to come in and gather in our house. And I remember staying up till late at night. They were always talking, and discussing politics, and everything else, like today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population of Male Werbcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the village itself, I would say, was maybe about 120, 130 families. And then surrounding, there were big farmers. And there were, around there, about maybe another 30, 40 home-- families in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And out of all these families, how many would have been Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were only-- in our village, there was my aunt and uncle. My mother's sister got married. This I remember. Her name was Beila. And she was married to a man by the name of Moishe, who was born in the village. And his parents lived not far from us. Their name was Golda and Itshak, his parents. And they were married. And they lived in our village of Male Werbcze. And they had two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were there any other Jewish families besides them?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was another lady, a widow, that we used to help out. She lived also in that village. She was all alone. I think she was a widow. But I never saw any kids. Maybe she was never married. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're telling-- you're telling me that there were only about two or three Jewish families.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two-- three Jewish families in our village, that's all. But all the other villages nearby had much more Yiddish-- Jewish-- many more Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the nearest village? How far away was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The nearest village was [? Korist, ?] where my grandparents lived. And there were probably about 20 families in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be about three to four kilometers. And another village, about six kilometers, which was [? Krichilsk. ?] And they had, I would say, about 30 families-- a big village. And [? Vilica ?] Werbcze, the next village, had also about 20 families. So during the holidays, we used to get together. Or Shabbos, they used to come to visit us, or we would visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember going to these towns and spending Shabbos there, or whatever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends? Who did you play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any Jewish friends. So we were playing always with the Ukrainian friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a girl that went to school with me. Her name was Katya. And one girl was Nastia Patrikei. And my friend Ivan Solovei-- that's the mayor's son. And I had some other friends that I don't remember off hand now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being close with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close, yes. Very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it matter that you were Jewish to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, I hardly ever felt-- I mean, there would be some kids called you a Jew or something. But not openly, like anybody to do any harm, or to persecute us, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any-- do you remember any incidents happening, either around specific Jewish holidays or around Christmastime, when it was their holiday? We didn't have any incidents of anti-Semitic incidents in my time. I remember my father used to say that there were some times when he was-- that's before the First World War-- he was attacked by the bands of [? Petlura, ?] which were the Ukrainian bands from Eastern Ukraine. And they beat him up. And as a matter of fact, his back was all, like, cut up with grooves in it. And he said these were all from beatings from the bandits, [? Petlura ?] bandits. But we didn't have any incidents until when the Germans arrived later on-- 1942-- in '41. Were you close with your sister and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very close, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite sibling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I used to play more with my older brother Yakov. He was only two years older. So I used to play with him and do some work around the house. I liked to do always something to fix around the house and so on. And he would be helping me to do some fixing around the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were handy--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as a kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always-- I liked always to do something, not to stand still, just always to build something or repair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a good student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fairly good, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your better-- what were some of your better subjects, or your more favorite ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, later on, when I went to a mechanical school-- actually, mechanics were always my favorite studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: But as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: But as a boy, nature. I liked nature always, farming and studying about farm. And since we lived in a village, we did a lot of planting in the school and during the harvest, so on-- before the harvest, studying about crops and everything else. And I liked it. But mainly, my-- I was good in poetry, for some reason, and singing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a favorite food that your mother would have made for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, like any other [LAUGHS] Jewish-- Eastern Jewish families, which would be chicken soup, and kugel, and gefilte fish, and all these [NON-ENGLISH] which were made in those days.
